title: Read the rule, audit the graph

objectives.statement: Interpret function notation as an input-output rule and determine domain, range, values, and compositions.
Read coordinates, intercepts, extrema, intervals, and axis scales from graph descriptions and equivalent value tables.
Calculate and interpret average rate of change as output change divided by input change with units.
Predict and verify translations, reflections, and stretches by tracking key inputs and outputs.

scenes.title: The endpoint is not the change
scenes.contentHtml:
The endpoint is not the change

A fictional process is recorded at two times:

Time, tt (min) Output, QQ (units)
0 12
4 28

A report calls the average rate 28/4=728/4=7 units per minute. But 28 is the endpoint, not the change. The output changed by 28−12=1628-12=16 units, so

ΔQΔt=28−124−0=4 units/min.\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}=\frac{28-12}{4-0}=4\text{ units/min}.

A graph is not decoration: each point asserts an input-output pair, and its axes determine what a slope or intercept means.

Starting check

Check
id check-starting-skills
  • In f(3)=11f(3)=11, which number is the input?
  • If a graph contains (2,−5)(2,-5), what does that say about the function?
  • Solve u−4=0u-4=0.

Feedback after attempting: The input is 3. The point (2,−5)(2,-5) says f(2)=−5f(2)=-5. The equation has solution u=4u=4 because adding 4 to both sides isolates uu.

If coordinate order is uncertain, always read (x,y)(x,y) as “input first, output second.” If solving the equation was difficult, review inverse operations in the algebra prerequisite before continuing.

Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-axis-quantity, claim-average-ratesources source-common-core-functions, source-nist-si-axis

The standards source supports function notation and average-rate interpretation; NIST supports unambiguous quantity and axis labeling. The table, rate error, and graph-audit sequence are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every graph relationship is supplied as coordinates, equations, interval language, or a value table. No task requires color, visual slope estimation, pointing, dragging, or timing.

scenes.title: A graph is a set of input-output claims
scenes.contentHtml:
A graph is a set of input-output claims

A function assigns exactly one output to each allowed input. In y=f(x)y=f(x), xx is an input, f(x)f(x) is its output, and the graph contains every ordered pair (x,f(x))(x,f(x)) in the domain.

Definition
id definition-domain-range

The domain is the set of allowed inputs. The range is the set of outputs the function actually produces over that domain.

For f(x)=x−2f(x)=\sqrt{x-2} over the reals, require x≥2x\ge2, so the domain begins at 2. Because a principal square root is nonnegative, the range begins at 0.

Composition

Composition feeds one output into another function:

(f∘g)(x)=f(g(x)).(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x)).

The domain must satisfy both stages. If f(u)=uf(u)=\sqrt u and g(x)=x−2g(x)=x-2, then (f∘g)(x)=x−2(f\circ g)(x)=\sqrt{x-2} requires x≥2x\ge2.

Reading graph structure

  • An xx-intercept has output 0.
  • A yy-intercept is f(0)f(0) when 0 is in the domain.
  • A maximum or minimum compares outputs over a stated domain.
  • Increasing means later inputs have larger outputs over an interval; it does not mean the outputs must be positive.
  • Average rate of change from aa to bb is [f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a)[f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for a≠ba\ne b.

Transformations

Starting from y=f(x)y=f(x):

  • f(x−h)+kf(x-h)+k shifts right by hh when h>0h>0 and left by |h||h| when h<0h<0; it shifts up by kk when k>0k>0 and down by |k||k| when k<0k<0;
  • −f(x)-f(x) reflects outputs across the horizontal axis;
  • f(−x)f(-x) reflects inputs across the vertical axis;
  • af(x)af(x) multiplies every output by aa.

Test a key input instead of relying on a slogan.

Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-axis-quantity, claim-transform-inference, claim-average-ratesources source-common-core-functions, source-nist-si-axis

The standards record supports function notation, graph features, average rate, and transformations; NIST supports explicit quantity and axis labels. Definitions, derivations, and examples are original mathematical synthesis.

scenes.title: Transform a parent rule and verify key points
scenes.contentHtml:
Transform a parent rule and verify key points

Consider the fictional response rule

g(t)=2(t−3)2+5.g(t)=2(t-3)^2+5.
Worked Example
id worked-example-quadratic-transform

1. Identify the parent. Start with f(t)=t2f(t)=t^2, whose minimum point is (0,0)(0,0).

2. Track transformations. Replacing tt by t−3t-3 moves the key input to 3. Multiplying outputs by 2 stretches them vertically. Adding 5 moves every output up 5.

3. Verify the minimum. At t=3t=3, g(3)=5g(3)=5. Because a square is nonnegative, g(t)≥5g(t)\ge5, so (3,5)(3,5) is the minimum point.

4. Build accessible points.

tt g(t)g(t)
2 7
3 5
4 7
6 23

5. Calculate average rate from 4 to 6.

g(6)−g(4)6−4=23−72=8.\frac{g(6)-g(4)}{6-4}=\frac{23-7}{2}=8.

6. Interpret. The average output change is 8 output units per input unit over that interval; it is not the instantaneous rate at every point.

Check
id check-key-input

Which substitution most directly verifies the claimed right shift: t=0t=0 or t=3t=3?

Feedback after attempting: t=3t=3 makes the transformed inner expression zero and locates the parent function's key input.

Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-transform-inference, claim-average-ratesources source-common-core-functions

The standards source supports the function, transformation, and average-rate relations. The response rule, value table, and interpretation are original.

scenes.title: Inside changes run opposite to the sign
scenes.contentHtml:
Inside changes run opposite to the sign

Check
id check-shift-prediction

Before reading the repair, predict whether f(x+3)f(x+3) shifts the parent graph left or right. Which new input recreates the parent input 0?

Misconception
id misconception-horizontal-shift

It is tempting to see f(x+3)f(x+3) and say “right 3” because the sign is positive. Instead, ask which new input makes the inside equal the parent key input 0:

x+3=0⇒x=−3.x+3=0\quad\Rightarrow\quad x=-3.

So the point that was at input 0 appears at input -3: the graph shifts left 3.

Outside changes act directly on outputs; inside changes alter which input reaches a given parent input. Testing a known point is safer than memorizing “opposite direction” without meaning.

Check
id check-shift-repair

Recheck the idea with p(x−5)p(x-5): which input recreates the parent input 0, and which way does the graph shift?

Feedback after attempting: Solve x−5=0x-5=0 to get x=5x=5. The parent point at input 0 therefore appears at input 5, so the graph shifts right 5.

Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-transform-inferencesources source-common-core-functions

The standards source supports reasoning about input transformations. The misconception, prediction, and key-input repair are original in expression.

scenes.title: Move among rule, table, and graph language
scenes.contentHtml:
Move among rule, table, and graph language

Attempt each check before continuing to its feedback.

1. Evaluate and interpret

Check
id check-practice-evaluate

For f(x)=3x−4f(x)=3x-4, find f(5)f(5) and name the graph point.

Feedback after attempting: f(5)=11f(5)=11, so (5,11)(5,11) lies on the graph.

2. Compose with a domain

Check
id check-practice-compose

Let f(u)=1/uf(u)=1/u and g(x)=x+2g(x)=x+2. Find (f∘g)(x)(f\circ g)(x) and its real domain.

Feedback after attempting: 1/(x+2)1/(x+2) with x≠−2x\ne-2.

3. Average rate

Check
id check-practice-rate

For q(t)=t2+1q(t)=t^2+1, find average rate from t=1t=1 to t=4t=4.

Feedback after attempting: [17−2]/[4−1]=5[17-2]/[4-1]=5 output units per input unit.

4. Transform

Check
id check-practice-transform

Describe h(x)=−3f(x−2)+1h(x)=-3f(x-2)+1 from ff.

Feedback after attempting: Shift right 2, multiply outputs by -3 (reflection across the horizontal axis and vertical stretch by 3), then shift up 1.

Recovery route

  • Coordinates reversed: restate input first, output second.
  • Endpoint divided by elapsed input: subtract both outputs before dividing.
  • Composition order reversed: compute the inside function first.
  • Horizontal shift reversed: solve for the input that recreates a known parent input.
  • Graph claim depends on appearance: rebuild it from labeled coordinates and intervals.
Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-axis-quantity, claim-transform-inference, claim-average-ratesources source-common-core-functions, source-nist-si-axis

The sources support the function, graph-labeling, transformation, and average-rate relations. All practice, feedback, and recovery routes are original.

scenes.title: Audit a transformed square-root rule
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit a transformed square-root rule

A fictional calibration curve uses distance xx in centimetres and signal h(x)h(x) in volts:

h(x)=−2x−1+6.h(x)=-2\sqrt{x-1}+6.
Check
id check-transfer-calibration

Without relying on a picture, determine its domain, range, starting point, direction of change, and the average rate from x=2x=2 to x=10x=10.

Feedback after a complete attempt

The square root requires x−1≥0x-1\ge0, so the domain is x≥1x\ge1. At x=1x=1, h(1)=6h(1)=6, giving starting point (1,6)(1,6).

As xx grows, x−1\sqrt{x-1} grows; multiplication by -2 makes the output decrease. Therefore the range is h(x)≤6h(x)\le6.

Values: h(2)=4h(2)=4 and h(10)=0h(10)=0. The average rate is

0−410−2=−12.\frac{0-4}{10-2}=-\frac12.

The average rate is −0.5-0.5 volts per centimetre. Its negative sign agrees with the decreasing rule. These statements completely specify the requested features in a screen-reader and text-only route.

Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-transform-inference, claim-average-ratesources source-common-core-functions

The standards source supports the function, transformation, graph-feature, and average-rate relations. The calibration rule, feature audit, and calculations are fictional original material.

scenes.title: Rebuild the graph audit
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the graph audit

  1. Name input and output quantities, units, and allowed domain.
  2. Treat each graph point as (x,f(x))(x,f(x)).
  3. Read intercepts and extrema against the stated domain and axis scale.
  4. Compute average rate from changes in both coordinates.
  5. Verify transformations with a key input and an accessible value table.

Retrieval

Check
id check-synthesis-retrieval
  • What makes a relation a function?
  • How do domain and range differ?
  • Why does f(x+3)f(x+3) shift a key point left?
  • Why is average rate not endpoint divided by elapsed input?

Feedback after attempting: A function assigns one output to each allowed input. The domain contains allowed inputs, whereas the range contains produced outputs. In f(x+3)f(x+3), the new input x=−3x=-3 recreates the parent input 0, so its key point moves left 3. Average rate compares output change with input change, so its numerator is f(b)−f(a)f(b)-f(a) rather than an endpoint output alone.

Complete assessment.json. If notation fails, return to the concept scene. If a transformation direction fails, use the key-input equation. If a graph interpretation depends only on visual shape, rewrite it as coordinates, intervals, and axis labels before retrying.

Source Note
claims claim-function-notation, claim-axis-quantity, claim-transform-inference, claim-average-ratesources source-common-core-functions, source-nist-si-axis

The synthesis combines sourced function, rate, transformation, and axis-labeling relations through an original graph-audit procedure.

assessment.title: Functions, graphs, and transformations mastery gate

commonErrors: Failing to group the negative input

items.prompt: Let f(u) = √u and g(x) = 5 - x. Find (f∘g)(x) and its real domain.

answer.reasoning: The output of g becomes the square-root argument and must be nonnegative.
commonErrors: Reversing the inequality when solving the domain condition

items.prompt: A function has f(2) = 11 and f(7) = -4. Find the average rate of change from 2 to 7 and state its meaning.

answer.correct: [-4 - 11]/[7 - 2] = -15/5 = -3 output units per input unit; over the interval, output decreases by 3 units on average for each one-unit increase in input.
answer.summary: Average rate = -3 output units per input unit.
answer.reasoning: Both coordinates must be differenced in the same order.
commonErrors: Dividing the endpoint output by endpoint input
Reversing only one difference and losing the sign

items.prompt: Relative to y = f(x), what does y = -2f(x + 4) + 3 do?

options.text: Left 4, reflect across the horizontal axis, vertical stretch 2, up 3
Right 4, reflect across the vertical axis, up 3

answer.summary: The graph shifts left 4; outputs are multiplied by -2 and then increased by 3.
answer.reasoning: The inside +4 changes which input reaches each parent input, while the outside -2 and +3 act directly on outputs.
commonErrors: Calling an output multiplier a horizontal stretch

items.prompt: An accessible text description of a graph says: horizontal axis time t (min), 2 min per tick; vertical axis volume V (mL), 5 mL per tick. Plotted points are given as (horizontal tick, vertical tick): (0,2), (1,4), (2,3), and (3,1). Convert the points to (t,V), state the y-intercept, identify each adjacent time interval over which V decreases, and calculate the average rate from t = 2 to t = 6.

answer.correct: The scaled points are (0,10), (2,20), (4,15), and (6,5), so the y-intercept is (0,10). V decreases from 2 to 4 min and from 4 to 6 min. The average rate from 2 to 6 min is (5 - 20)/(6 - 2) = -15/4 = -3.75 mL/min.
answer.summary: Scaled points (0,10), (2,20), (4,15), (6,5); y-intercept (0,10); decreasing on [2,4] and [4,6]; average rate -3.75 mL/min from 2 to 6.
answer.reasoning: Convert tick positions using each axis scale before reading the intercept, intervals, or endpoint changes.
commonErrors: Treating tick positions as coordinate values
Applying one axis scale to both axes
Dividing the endpoint volume by elapsed time

items.prompt: A fictional response is p(x) = -0.5(x + 2)² + 8 over all real x. Without drawing, describe transformations from x², identify the maximum point and range, find both x-intercepts, calculate average rate from x = -2 to x = 0, and provide a three-row value table that verifies your claims.

answer.correct: From x²: shift left 2, reflect across the horizontal axis, vertically compress by factor 0.5, then shift up 8. The maximum is (-2,8) and range p ≤ 8. For intercepts, 0 = -0.5(x+2)²+8 gives (x+2)²=16, so x=-6 or 2. p(-2)=8 and p(0)=6, so average rate is (6-8)/(0-(-2))=-1. A verifying table may use (-6,0), (-2,8), and (2,0).
answer.summary: Maximum (-2,8), range p ≤ 8, intercepts (-6,0) and (2,0), average rate -1.
answer.reasoning: The squared term is nonnegative; the negative coefficient turns the vertex into a maximum, and direct substitution verifies the symmetric key points.
commonErrors: Dividing p(0) by 2 instead of using output change

criteria.description: Substitutes the complete negative input and obtains 5.
Interprets sign, interval, and output-per-input units.
Identifies left shift, reflection/stretch, and upward shift in order.
Converts both axis scales and identifies the y-intercept.
Identifies both decreasing intervals and calculates the labeled average rate.

creators: National Institute of Standards and Technology

agentAccess.notes: NIST rights, technical-series licensing, and robots routes were checked before access; no agent restriction or access barrier was found.

claims.statement: Function notation associates each allowed input with one output, and composition evaluates one function's output as another function's input where both domains permit it.
claims.scope: Real-valued functions and compositions used in introductory quantitative reasoning.
claims.uncertainty: Multivalued complex relations and advanced function spaces are outside scope.

claims.statement: Graph axes and table headings should identify the represented quantity or numerical value and its unit unambiguously so coordinates and rates can be interpreted correctly.
claims.scope: Quantitative graphs and tables used in this lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Clear labeling does not by itself make a selected visualization or model valid.

claims.statement: Translations, reflections, and stretches of a function can be derived by tracking how changed inputs reach parent inputs and how changed outputs are subsequently transformed.
claims.scope: Algebraic transformations of the real functions used in the lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Graph appearance can also depend on the viewing window, which does not alter the underlying function.

claims.statement: For distinct inputs a and b, average rate of change is the output change f(b)-f(a) divided by the input change b-a and is interpreted in output units per input unit over that interval.
claims.scope: Average rate of change for real-valued functions over intervals with distinct endpoints.
claims.uncertainty: This interval average does not assert a constant or instantaneous rate within the interval.

terms.definition: A rule or relation assigning exactly one output to each allowed input.
The set of inputs for which a function is defined in the stated context.
The set of outputs a function produces over its declared domain.
An operation that uses the output of one function as the input of another.
Output change divided by input change over a specified interval, with the corresponding output-per-input units.
A systematic change to function inputs or outputs that translates, reflects, or stretches its graph.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The graph-audit structure, process table, quadratic example, shift misconception, square-root transfer, all equations, tables, feedback, recovery, and assessments are original. All applied settings are fictional. The Common Core standards support function, graph-feature, composition, average-rate, and transformation relations; NIST provides factual axis-labeling guidance. No source expression, graph, table, or example is copied or adapted.

The cited standards and NIST access and terms routes were checked before use on 2026-07-23 and 2026-07-22, respectively. No source-specific agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
